data.title: BofA’s Hartnett: Investors prepare for ‘buy the expectation, sell the fact’
data.description:
Bank of America’s 2025 outlook signals a major pivot in investment strategies.

Michael Hartnett, investment strategist at Bank of America, said the bank has shifted from the 2020’s view of “anything but bonds” and “anywhere but China” towards preferences for bonds (SPLB), (BND), (BSV) over equities (SP500), (DJI), (COMP:IND), international stocks (ACWX) over U.S., and gold (NYSEARCA:GLD) over U.S. dollar (DXY).

This strategic realignment stems from anticipated clashes between U.S. exceptionalism and emerging populist policies that could impact markets.

The pace of U.S. nominal GDP growth, which surged 50% over the past five years, is expected to slow significantly, Hartnett warned.

The 2025 policy landscape appears increasingly restrictive as the Federal Reserve is projected to halt rate cuts while government spending could stagnate or decline by $50B.

Though the effective U.S. tariff rate may decrease overall, the strategist highlighted that potential tariffs on China (30%) and the EU (10%) would still represent “equivalent to >$600B rise in tariff taxation.”

These fiscal constraints could substantially reshape market dynamics through 2025.

Hartnett suggested that currently oversold equities have “correctly front-run Q2 trade deals/lower tariffs,” but cautions investors to prepare for a “buy the expectation, sell the fact” scenario when trade agreements materialize.

The strategist recommends maintaining short positions on the U.S. dollar (DXY) until the Fed resumes rate cuts and holding 5-year U.S. Treasuries (US5Y) until potential GOP tax cuts become formalized.

He said that macro catalysts that could fuel further market gains include the “3Cs,” a China deal, rate cuts (with China doing the biggest easing), and a strong consumer.

The primary risks to markets stem from potential “asset prices” collapse and subsequent deleveraging contagion, he added. This concern is amplified by recent data showing global debt reaching an all-time high $324T in the first quarter, a vulnerability that could trigger widespread market disruption if asset values decline precipitously.

data.title: New York Fed plans to offer morning repo operations soon
data.description:
After testing the use of a Standing Repo Facility (SRF) in a morning auction, the Federal Reserve Bank of New York plans to make early-settlement SRF auctions part of its regular daily schedule in addition to the existing afternoon settlements "in the not-too-distant future," it said on Friday.

In 2021, the New York Fed started SRFs to serve as a backstop in money markets. It essentially offers very short-term loans (overnight) to financial institutions, providing the cash to ensure smooth operations and liquid markets. The Fed buys a security from an eligible counterparty and agrees to sell it back the next day. The difference between the purchase prices and the sale price implies a rate of interest earned by the Fed on the transaction.

"These early-settlement auctions, combined with the current afternoon auctions, will enhance the effectiveness of the SRF as a tool for monetary policy implementation and market functioning," said Roberto Perli, the New York Fed's manager of the System Open Market Account.

The existing SRFs typically take place every business day from 1:30 PM to 1:45 PM ET.

The SOMA desk conducted an early-settlement SRF small-value exercise for the first time on March 5 to test capabilities. After a positive assessment of that exercise, it then conducted early-settlement SRF operations each day from March 27 through April 2, in addition to the regular afternoon operations.

"The operations went smoothly and were regarded as successful," Perli said.

"Our market outreach following the March quarter-end revealed that primary dealers see the early-settlement SRF operations as an enhancement that increases the likelihood that the SRF will be used when economically convenient to do so," he added.

And while the early settlements will enhance the use of SRF, there's still room for improvement, he said. "Where possible, Federal Reserve staff will continue to look for ways to improve the efficacy of the SRF."

data.title: Assured guaranty anticipates growing demand for bond insurance amid market volatility
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Assured Guaranty Ltd. (AGO) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Dominic Frederico highlighted a solid performance in Q1 2025, with adjusted operating income per share reaching $3.18, a significant improvement from $1.96 in the prior year. Key valuation metrics also achieved record highs, including adjusted operating shareholders' equity at $117.40 and adjusted book value at $172.79 per share.
  • The company reported $39 million in new business production, primarily from U.S. public finance, which contributed 65% of the total. Global structured finance and non-U.S. public finance also provided notable contributions.
  • A $103 million pretax gain was recognized from the resolution of litigation with Lehman Brothers International, demonstrating the company’s commitment to defending its legal rights.
  • CFO Ben Rosenblum emphasized the contribution of fee-based earnings from the company’s 30% ownership in Sound Point, which added $13 million to income during the quarter.

Outlook

  • Management expressed confidence in increasing demand for their guaranty products amid heightened market volatility. The company sees opportunities to expand their core products and expects demand to grow further in the current economic climate.
  • CEO Frederico referenced the potential for increased secondary market activities, citing $376 million in insured par this quarter—more than the entirety of 2024—and emphasized ongoing investments in technology to enhance secondary market penetration.
  • The company highlighted plans to expand in international markets, particularly in Continental Europe, with a recent financial guarantee for a French infrastructure operator marking a strategic milestone.

Financial Results

  • Adjusted operating income for Q1 2025 was $162 million, driven by a $103 million litigation gain. Excluding litigation benefits, scheduled net earned premiums and credit derivative revenues increased by 7%.
  • The investment portfolio performed strongly, generating $59 million in income from alternative investments, with an inception-to-date annualized return of 13%.
  • Net investment income on fixed maturity and short-term investments totaled $90 million, reflecting stable performance.
  • Share repurchases during the quarter amounted to 1.3 million shares for $120 million, contributing to new highs in adjusted operating shareholders' equity and adjusted book value per share.

Q&A

  • Marissa Lobo, UBS: Asked about UK water sector exposures, including Thames Water. CEO Frederico and CFO Rosenblum discussed reserving scenarios and expressed confidence that no losses would be incurred, emphasizing their position as senior creditors and recent liquidity planning.
  • Tommy McJoynt, KBW: Inquired about the competitive environment in secondary markets and the company’s technology investments. COO Rob Bailenson noted minimal competition and highlighted enhanced systems enabling real-time pricing and execution efficiencies.
  • Another question focused on the Asset Management segment’s strong contribution for the quarter. CFO Rosenblum attributed the performance to Sound Point’s back-end-loaded revenue patterns and emphasized its role in diversifying revenue streams.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst sentiment was neutral to slightly positive, with a focus on specific credit exposures and strategic initiatives. Questions reflected a mix of curiosity about growth opportunities and concerns regarding potential risks in certain credit exposures.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, emphasizing strategic preparedness and robust financial positioning. Frederico’s statements showcased confidence in the company’s ability to navigate volatile markets and leverage opportunities.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Adjusted operating income per share rose from $1.27 in Q4 2024 to $3.18 in Q1 2025, reflecting gains from litigation and improved business metrics.
  • New business production remained steady, with a notable increase in secondary market activities compared to prior quarters.
  • Management’s tone in Q1 2025 was more optimistic, citing tangible progress in international markets and technology advancements for secondary business growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Potential delays in resolving PREPA issues contributed to economic loss development of $48 million.
  • UK water sector exposures were discussed, with management outlining multiple scenarios but downplaying the likelihood of significant losses.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the impact of market volatility and economic uncertainty on credit exposures, which management addressed with assurances of preparedness and strategic focus.

Final Takeaway

Assured Guaranty Ltd. reported a strong first quarter for 2025, driven by litigation gains, solid business production, and robust investment performance. With record highs in key valuation metrics and increased demand for its guaranty products amid market volatility, the company appears well-positioned for continued growth. Management’s confidence in their strategic initiatives and ability to mitigate risks underscores a positive outlook for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Goldman sachs bdc outlines $13.20 NAV and $0.42 NII for Q1 2025 amid portfolio adjustments
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Goldman Sachs BDC (GSBD) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Co-CEO Alex Chi highlighted the resilience of the Goldman Sachs BDC portfolio amid challenging macroeconomic conditions. He stated that only 3% of the portfolio's fair value is highly exposed to tariffs, as most companies are U.S.-based and operate within service-oriented industries such as software, healthcare, and business services.
  • Chi discussed the impact of tariffs and macroeconomic uncertainty on deal flow, noting a delay in M&A activity resurgence. However, he emphasized that deals in process have shifted their financing back to direct lending.
  • Co-CEO David Miller reported $87.8 million in new investment commitments in Q1, with 100% in first-lien loans. He noted the company’s role as lead arranger in 72% of these deals. Notable investments included Vermont Information Processing and Celero Commerce.
  • CFO Stanley Matuszewski reported net investment income per share of $0.42 and a net asset value (NAV) per share of $13.20, reflecting a 1.6% decrease due to a special dividend and realized and unrealized losses.

Outlook

  • Chi reiterated the company’s focus on maintaining a low-to-mid 9% yield for new investments and leveraging the Goldman Sachs ecosystem for origination opportunities. He mentioned optimism for increased deal volume driven by private equity dynamics and market fundamentals.
  • The Board declared a base dividend of $0.32 per share and a supplemental dividend of $0.05 per share, reflecting the company’s adjusted dividend structure.

Financial Results

  • The company reported total portfolio investments at fair value of $3.38 billion, with 96.1% in senior secured loans and 90.7% in first-lien positions.
  • Weighted average yields of debt and income-producing investments stood at 10.8%, slightly lower than the previous quarter’s 11.2%.
  • Non-accrual investments decreased to 1.9% of the total portfolio by fair value.
  • GAAP net investment income totaled $49.6 million, with a decrease in total investment income to $96.9 million compared to $103.8 million in Q4 2024.

Q&A

  • Derek Hewett, Bank of America, inquired about the 40-basis-point decline in portfolio yield. Chi and Miller attributed this to the exit of non-accrual positions with high coupons and noted an increase in average spreads for new deals.
  • Hewett asked about the impact of tariff exposure on investments. Chi clarified that identified high-exposure loans were conservatively categorized but had not yet shown performance issues.
  • Analysts queried the stability of the dividend structure. Management confirmed that the $0.32 per share base dividend was modeled to account for market conditions, including future refinancing costs.
  • Questions about portfolio composition highlighted a reduction in annual recurring revenue loans, with stricter criteria for new investments.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed concerns about portfolio yield and tariff exposure, reflecting a slightly negative tone. Questions also showed skepticism toward dividend sustainability and potential risks from broader market conditions.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, especially in discussing the portfolio’s U.S.-centric focus and improved credit quality metrics. They emphasized proactive measures to mitigate risks and highlighted positive developments like reduced non-accrual investments.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence remained steady, with a focus on leveraging the Goldman Sachs platform for deal origination and portfolio optimization.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • NAV per share decreased from $13.41 in Q4 2024 to $13.20 in Q1 2025, impacted by dividends and market adjustments.
  • Weighted average yields declined from 11.2% to 10.8% quarter-over-quarter, reflecting portfolio adjustments.
  • Non-accrual investments improved, decreasing to 1.9% at fair value compared to 2% in the previous quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from dividend adjustments last quarter to portfolio yield and tariff impacts this quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified macroeconomic uncertainties, including tariffs and supply chain exposures, as potential risks. Only 3% of the portfolio was flagged for high exposure.
  • Analysts questioned the sustainability of the dividend structure and potential impacts of future refinancing costs.

Final Takeaway

Goldman Sachs BDC displayed resilience in a challenging economic environment, with a focus on first-lien investments and leveraging its broader platform for origination opportunities. While NAV and investment yields showed slight declines, management emphasized portfolio quality improvements and proactive risk mitigation. Analysts posed critical questions on yield stability and tariff exposure, but the company’s emphasis on strategic adjustments and a stable dividend policy suggests a commitment to navigating macroeconomic challenges effectively.
